16: naos theou este kai to pneuma tou theou oikei en hymin (naos theou este kai to pneuma tou theou oikei en hymin): 'you are the temple of God and the Spirit of God dwells in you.' Naos is the inner sanctuary (the Most Holy Place and Holy Place), not merely the outer court (hieron). Paul uses the corporate 'you' (plural in Greek) — the community together is the naos, the place of divine habitation. This radically relocates the dwelling of God: not in the Jerusalem temple but in the community gathered around Christ. The destruction of this community-temple by division and immorality (1 Corinthians 3:17) is therefore as serious as desecrating the Jerusalem sanctuary.
1: σαρκίνοις (sarkinois = made of flesh) — the adjective describes constitution, not conduct: Paul addresses them as people who are, by constitution, fleshly (infants in Christ). The contrast in 1 Corinthians 3:3 shifts to σαρκικοί (sarkikoi = flesh-dominated, acting according to flesh) — now a description of chosen conduct. The move from what they were (understandably fleshly as new believers) to what they still are (inexcusably flesh-dominated given time passed) is the accusatory pivot of this section.
2: γάλα ὑμᾶς ἐπότισα, οὐ βρῶμα (I fed you milk, not solid food) — the feeding-metaphor distinguishes elementary instruction (milk) from mature teaching (broma = solid food). Epotisa (aorist: I gave to drink) is the technical word for providing drink to someone, used also for watering plants (1 Corinthians 3:6, 7-8). The metaphor fuses parental feeding and agricultural irrigation in a single letter-unit.
3: σαρκικοί ἐστε (you are fleshly) — the present tense is judgmental: this is their current state. Zēlos kai eris (jealousy and strife) are the presenting symptoms of fleshly operation. The rhetorical question ouchi sarkikoi este kai kata anthrōpon peripatete? (are you not fleshly and are you not walking according to the merely human standard?) uses the Pauline kata anthrōpon (according to the human) as the mark of operating below the Spirit's level.
4: ἄνθρωποί ἐστε (you are merely human beings) — the demonstrative is dismissive: mere humans, operating at the merely human level. The factions are identified as the symptom; the underlying disease is operating at the human level rather than the Spirit level. Paul does not say they are not Christians but that they are functioning like people who have no Spirit.
5: διάκονοι (diakonoi = servants, deacons) — the lowest service category, distinct from the master or the overseer. Paul and Apollos are not party leaders but ministerial workers through whom faith came. Ὡς ὁ κύριος ἔδωκεν ἑκάστῳ (as the Lord gave to each one) — aorist, a completed assignment. The diversity of ministry is divinely assigned distribution, not competitive differentiation.
6: ἐγὼ ἐφύτευσα, Ἀπολλῶς ἐπότισεν (I planted, Apollos watered) — both verbs are aorist: completed historical acts with specific referents. The imperfect ηὔξανεν (was causing to grow) for God's action stands in contrast: not a single act but the continuous, ongoing, sustaining growth that only God provides. The tense contrast is theologically deliberate: human ministry is momentary; divine growth is continuous.
7: οὔτε ὁ φυτεύων ἐστίν τι οὔτε ὁ ποτίζων (neither the planter nor the waterer is anything) — the negation of being (is anything) radically de-centers the human worker. The present tense denotes the permanent status: the planter's identity as nothing-in-themselves is ongoing, not just situational. Only God who causes growth is anything, and he remains the subject of the imperfect auxanōn (the one growing).
8: ὁ φυτεύων δὲ καὶ ὁ ποτίζων ἕν εἰσιν (the one who plants and the one who waters are one) — not one person but one in status and direction. They will each receive their own reward (idion misthon) according to their own labor (idion kopon): individual accountability to God, not competitive comparison with each other.
9: θεοῦ γεώργιον, θεοῦ οἰκοδομή ἐστε (you are God's field, God's building) — the genitives are emphatic by fronting: God's field, God's building — not Paul's, not Apollos's. The double metaphor (agricultural → architectural) shifts the imagery for the section that follows. The community belongs to God; the builders are stewards, not owners.
10: ἀρχιτέκτων (architekton = master builder, architect) — the root of our English word. Paul lays the foundation with the skill of a master craftsman. Ἄλλος δὲ ἐποικοδομεῖ (another builds on it) — present tense: ongoing building activity of others. The warning hekastos blepetō pōs epoikodomei (let each one take care how he builds) is present imperative: a rule for ongoing building practice.
11: θεμέλιον γὰρ ἄλλον οὐδεὶς δύναται θεῖναι (for no one is able to lay another foundation) — the double negation (allon = other/different + oudeis dunatai = no one is able) is absolute: the impossibility is not practical but ontological. Christ is not one available foundation among others but the only possible one. Ὃς ἔστιν Ἰησοῦς Χριστός (which is Jesus Christ) — the relative clause identifies the foundation with the full messianic name.
12: χρυσόν, ἄργυρον, λίθους τιμίους, ξύλον, χόρτον, καλάμην (gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw) — the three durable materials (gold, silver, precious stones) and three combustible materials (wood, hay, straw) are not identified with specific teachings or errors. The test will reveal which category each building component belongs to; speculative identification of what each material represents misses the point — the test is eschatological, not hermeneutical.
13: ἡ ἡμέρα (the Day) — the definite article marks this as the eschatological Day of the Lord; Paul's readers needed no explanation of which Day. Ἐν πυρὶ ἀποκαλύπτεται (it will be revealed through fire) — the fire is not punitive first but revelatory: the Day's fire reveals what kind of work it is. The testing-metaphor draws on Malachi 3:2-3 (the refiner's fire). The distinction between the builder's work being burned (katakahēsetai) and the builder being saved (sōthēsetai) is a crucial soteriological point: salvation is not based on the quality of the ministerial work.
14: εἰ τινος τὸ ἔργον μενεῖ ὃ ἐπῳκοδόμησεν, μισθὸν λήψεται (if anyone's work which they built remains, they will receive a reward) — first-class conditional (if, and it does): those whose work survives the eschatological fire will receive reward. Misthon (reward) is the standard term for wages due for work performed — eschatological recompense, not salvation itself.
15: αὐτὸς δὲ σωθήσεται, οὕτως δὲ ὡς διὰ πυρός (but he himself will be saved, but as through fire) — the famous "saved, but so as through fire" clause. The builder whose work is consumed will be saved anyway — but with the experience of losing everything one built. The construction hōs dia pyros (as if through fire) is an adverbial comparison: the manner of salvation is like escaping a burning building with nothing but one's life.
17: φθερεῖ αὐτόν (will destroy him) — future indicative: God's judgment on those who destroy the community is certain and proportionate: destroyer destroyed. The chiastic structure: if anyone destroys God's temple / God will destroy that person / for God's temple is sacred / which [temple] you are — the chiasm identifies the community as the sacred object of divine protection.
18: μηδεὶς ἑαυτὸν ἐξαπατάτω (let no one deceive himself) — present imperative with mēdeis (no one): an absolute prohibition. Exapataō (deceive thoroughly, from ex-apataō) intensifies the deception warning. The condition for gaining the wisdom of this age is becoming mōros (a fool) by the standards of that same age — the cross-pattern applied to epistemology.
19: δρασσόμενος (drassomenos = catching, gripping) — a vivid present participle from drassomai: to grab, to seize. The image is of divine hands actively catching the wise in mid-cunning. Panourgia (craftiness, cunning) is a loaded word: pan + ergon (all-deed) = willing to do anything, totally unprincipled cleverness. The same word describes the serpent's approach to Eve (2 Corinthians 11:3; Genesis 3:1 LXX).
20: διαλογισμοὶ τῶν σοφῶν (reasonings of the wise) — the noun dialogismoi (reasonings, debates, inner calculations) carries a slightly negative charge in Paul: the inner calculations of people relying on their own rational framework. Mataios (futile, empty, worthless) — the word used in the LXX for hebel (vanity/vapor in Ecclesiastes) and for idol-worship as empty/futile activity.
21: ὥστε μηδεὶς καυχάσθω ἐν ἀνθρώποις (so then let no one boast in human beings) — the present imperative kauchāsthō again: no ongoing boasting in human leaders. The counter-declaration panta gar hymōn estin (for all things are yours) is the positive: instead of the community belonging to the leaders, the leaders belong to the community. The ownership is reversed.
22: Παῦλος ἢ Ἀπολλῶς ἢ Κηφᾶς — the three human leaders who served as the faction-labels are listed as among the things that belong to the community, along with kosmos, zōē, thanatos, ta enestōta, ta mellonta (world, life, death, the present, the future). The cosmic scope of the community's inheritance makes factional boasting about any single leader absurd: the community that possesses the world should not divide over who preaches best.
23: ὑμεῖς δὲ Χριστοῦ, Χριστὸς δὲ θεοῦ (you are of Christ, and Christ is of God) — the chain of possession concludes. The community belongs to Christ (not to Paul, Apollos, or Cephas); Christ belongs to God (not as an independent source of salvation but as the Son who executes the Father's will). The chain grounds all authority in the Father and establishes Christ as the only legitimate head of the community.
